CONCLUS I ONS
1. Paynents to an elected state officer of intrastate travel
and reasonaLle accommodations j-n connection with a speech currently
ai" not cons.idered payments, gifts or income under Regulation

However both of these regulations wiII be presented to

official 
enjoys no benefits and over which the official 
has no
discretion or control are not disclosable gifts to the official.
l. 
If an official

the gift is likely to be deened a gift to the official' 
If the

is necessarily a

DTSCUSSION
Effective January 1, 1991, the Legislature enacted a series of
statutes that timit or ban certain gifts or honoraria made to
public officials. 
The Act now provides:
No locaI elected officeholder shalI accept any
honoraria for any speech given, article published,
or attendance at any public or private conference,
convention, rneeting, social event, mea1, or Iike
gathering, or any gifts, 
from any single source.
wnicn is in excess of one thousand dollars
(51,ooo), in any calendar year, excePt
reimbursement for actual travel expenses and
reasonable subsistence in connection therewith.
Section 89501.
(a) No elected state of f .icer nay accept an
honorarium.
(b) "Honorariumrr neans, except as provided in
subdivision (c), any Payment rnade in consideration

In our conversations of Febru ary 28, 1991, and March 1, 1991,

to others' I
can only confirm the advi.ce with a qualifi.er.

to the donor that tickets to an event be given to other persons

I stated that generally they would not be treated as gifts to the
official. 
This Ietter confirns that general conclusion. Ho!,ever,
r also cautioned that the conclusion uas necessarily based on the

advice nust be considered on a case-by-case basis.
